title: NATO's $50 Billion Procurement Surge and the New Arms Geography
subhead: The Ankara summit produced over $50 billion in defense deals, a push for European weapons production, and a Patriot license for Ukraine — while Russia banned diesel exports after Ukrainian drone strikes gutted its refining capacity, and the Pentagon pivoted toward cheaper, expendable drones after losing $1 billion in Reapers to Iran.

sections.heading: NATO Ankara Summit: $50 Billion in Deals, 5% Spending Pledge, and a Transatlantic Industrial Shift
sections.body:
The NATO summit in Ankara produced the alliance's largest-ever defense industry showcase, with officials touting more than $50 billion in procurement and industrial agreements. The White House fact sheet listed $3 billion in specific US defense contracts, including several that shift production to Europe for the first time.

Lockheed Martin will establish a PAC-3 missile sustainment facility in Europe and partner with Germany's Rheinmetall on European production of the Army Tactical Missile System (ATACMS). RTX and the Department of War will launch an AMRAAM feasibility study for expanded European production. Germany and the Netherlands will jointly procure Raytheon Stinger missiles with European production as a condition, aiming to double Stinger output by 2030. Boeing and Rheinmetall-Italy will explore expanding Small Diameter Bomb (SDB-I) production for Europe. Anduril committed Barracuda-500 missiles for Poland, with a new in-country production line. Northrop Grumman signed letters of intent with 10 nations for MQ-4C Triton maritime surveillance drones.

The summit also reaffirmed the 5% of GDP defense spending target by 2035, pledged at last year's summit. According to the White House, European allies spent $120 billion more in the past year alone, and NATO allies procured over $54 billion in US defense equipment in 2025. The Department of War has returned US troop levels in Europe to pre-2022 levels, explicitly shifting conventional defense responsibility to European allies.

Turkish President Erdogan used the host platform to urge allies to lift defense industry restrictions within NATO — a direct reference to US arms export controls that have complicated Turkey's defense procurement.
sections.whyItMatters: The Ankara summit marks a structural shift: European allies are not just spending more, they are building more on their own soil. Moving PAC-3 sustainment, ATACMS production, Stinger manufacturing, and SDB assembly to Europe addresses chronic supply-chain bottlenecks while creating European industrial capacity that reduces dependency on transatlantic logistics. The procurement coalition model — allies pooling orders to lower unit costs — is NATO's answer to the production-volume problem exposed by the Ukraine war.

sections.heading: Trump Pledges Patriot License for Ukraine and F-35s for Turkey
sections.body:
In a bilateral meeting with Zelenskyy on the summit sidelines, Trump announced the US would grant Ukraine a license to produce Patriot air defense interceptors domestically. 'We're going to give a license to you to make Patriots. That's pretty cool, right?' Trump told Zelenskyy. He acknowledged the manufacturer hasn't been informed yet but said 'that'll work out all right.'

When asked about providing Patriot interceptors up front while production ramps up, Trump was blunt: 'We have Patriots, but we don't have that many. We need them for ourselves, too.' The license won't yield interceptors quickly — the NYT noted it 'could take years' given Ukraine's limited industrial base and the complexity of Patriot manufacturing.

Separately, Trump said he would consider allowing Turkey to purchase F-35 jets again and would lift US sanctions on Turkey's defense procurement. Turkey was expelled from the F-35 program in 2019 over its purchase of Russia's S-400 system. The potential reversal is a major concession to Erdogan and a signal that the US is prioritizing alliance cohesion over the S-400 dispute.
sections.whyItMatters: The Patriot license for Ukraine addresses a real crisis — global interceptor shortages mean Ukraine cannot defend its cities adequately — but production timelines mean this is a long-term bet, not immediate relief. The F-35 signal to Turkey is more immediately consequential: if carried through, it reverses one of the most consequential US-Turkey defense disputes in decades and reopens a major military export channel.

sections.heading: Pentagon's Massed Modular Aircraft: Cheaper Drones to Replace $1 Billion in Lost Reapers
sections.body:
The Pentagon's Defense Innovation Unit issued a solicitation for the Massed Modular Aircraft (MMA) project — a direct response to the loss of nearly 30 MQ-9 Reaper drones worth roughly $1 billion during the Iran conflict. The Air Force's Reaper fleet has dropped to about 135 aircraft, according to Air Force Times, with General Atomics having stopped manufacturing the type for the US military in 2025.

The DIU solicitation calls for drones carrying 2,800-pound payloads with a combat radius of at least 2,300 nautical miles (or 8,000 nm on one-way strike missions), speeds above 200 mph, and the ability to operate from 6,000-foot or improvised runways. The project targets 20 mission-ready aircraft by FY2031, with full-scale prototype flight testing within 21 months of contract award. A single operator should be able to control several aircraft simultaneously.

The solicitation explicitly frames the problem as unsustainable: 'The Joint Force's reliance on low-density, high-value exquisite (>$30 million) manned and unmanned aircraft is unsustainable against adversaries utilizing layered defenses enabled by increasingly low-cost antiaircraft capabilities.' Iran's IRGC claimed to have shot down yet another Reaper on July 8.

The Pentagon has requested roughly $54 billion for drones and autonomous systems in its FY2027 budget — an amount Ars Technica noted rivals Ukraine's entire wartime military budget.
sections.whyItMatters: The MMA program is the Pentagon's most explicit acknowledgment yet that the era of $30-50 million individually exquisite UAVs is over against peer or near-peer air defenses. The shift toward expendable, mass-producible platforms reflects lessons from both Ukraine and Iran: in both conflicts, the defender ran out of interceptors before the attacker ran out of drones, but the cost exchange ratio was devastating when the drones were expensive Reapers. Whether industry can deliver long-range, heavy-payload drones at prices low enough for mass production remains the open question.

sections.heading: Russia Bans Diesel Exports as Ukrainian Drone Strikes Cripple Refining Capacity
sections.body:
On July 8, Russia imposed a ban on diesel fuel exports and began importing fuel from Belarus and Kazakhstan, according to Deputy Prime Minister Alexander Novak. The move came after a sustained Ukrainian drone campaign knocked out approximately 25% of Russia's total refining capacity compared to the previous year, with the Moscow refinery alone — which supplied 40% of the capital region's fuel — hit and forced to halt operations.

The crisis has led to fuel rationing in over 20 Russian regions (Ukrainian estimates say 60 are affected), with limits of 20 liters per vehicle and bans on canister purchases. The Kremlin is subsidizing pump prices 30-50% below market rates through a dampener mechanism costing at least 200 billion rubles ($3 billion) per month. Black market rates in the hardest-hit areas run two to three times official prices. Russia has also lowered environmental standards from Euro-5 to Euro-3.

Kazakhstan introduced strict entry limits on Russian vehicles to once per day, citing fuel smuggling concerns. The export ban further restricts a commodity that was already under pressure from Western sanctions on Russian energy exports.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine's drone campaign against Russian refining infrastructure has achieved what sanctions alone could not: a genuine domestic fuel crisis in Russia. The diesel export ban is a direct economic cost of the war reaching Russian civilians and industry. The 25% refining capacity reduction — if sustained — erodes a key Russian revenue stream and complicates military logistics. This is a case where drone warfare is producing strategic effects that rival conventional military operations.

sections.heading: US Plans Permanent Marine Corps Weapons Stockpile in Australia
sections.body:
US Navy tender documents reveal plans for a permanent Marine Corps weapons stockpile in Victoria, southeastern Australia, with $30 million allocated for warehouses and offices at the Bandiana military base. The facility, expected to be operational by 2028, will store crew-served weapons and combat-ready supplies with about 110 engineers, mechanics, and logistics specialists managing the site.

Equipment will initially be stored in Melbourne before transferring to dedicated US warehouses at Bandiana. The Pentagon has requested $500 million from Congress for FY2027 to enhance prepositioning across the Indo-Pacific. The location in southeastern Australia places the stockpile beyond the range of most Chinese ballistic missiles, according to a Lowy Institute report — northern Australia is within range of Chinese missiles deployed from South China Sea outposts.

Australian Defence Minister Richard Marles described the plans as part of a 'growing US footprint' in Australia and important for national security.
sections.whyItMatters: The Bandiana stockpile is a logistics investment that makes US force posture in the Indo-Pacific more credible. Prepositioned equipment means Marines can deploy from Australia with their gear already in place rather than waiting for sealift — a critical advantage in a Taiwan contingency scenario. The deliberate choice of southeastern Australia, beyond Chinese missile range, signals that the Pentagon takes the vulnerability of forward bases seriously and is planning for a conflict where those bases could come under attack.

sections.heading: India's ₹10 Trillion Post-Sindoor Arms Buying Spree
sections.body:
India's Defence Acquisition Council has approved roughly ₹10 lakh crore ($119 billion) in procurement since Operation Sindoor, the military confrontation with Pakistan that accelerated India's defense spending. The approvals span the full spectrum: 114 Rafale fighters (acceptance of necessity granted in February), five additional S-400 battery orders, six more P-8I maritime patrol aircraft, loitering munitions for artillery regiments, Astra Mk-2 missiles, SPICE-1000 guidance kits, and anti-drone systems.

The most recent DAC meeting on July 3 approved ₹52,000 crore ($6.2 billion) in proposals emphasizing UAVs, electronic warfare systems, anti-UAV systems, and 2,300 man-portable anti-tank guided missiles with 100 launchers. The Print reports that this is the most conservative batch so far compared to earlier post-Sindoor rounds.

India's annual capital expenditure budget for all three services is roughly ₹2.19 lakh crore ($23 billion), meaning the approved programs carry a long procurement tail — most won't be delivered for years. The mix ranges from fully Indian-designed systems to foreign government-to-government purchases, with the first Rafale squadron likely arriving in flyaway condition.
sections.whyItMatters: India's post-Sindoor procurement wave is one of the largest single-nation defense buying programs underway anywhere. The emphasis on air defense (S-400, surface-to-air missiles), loitering munitions, and anti-drone systems reflects the lessons India drew from the Pakistan confrontation. The sheer scale of approvals against a much smaller annual budget means India is making choices about priorities that will shape its military for a decade — and the tension between 'Make in India' ambitions and the urgency of operational gaps remains unresolved.

sections.heading: Trump Threatens Spain With Trade Cutoff Over NATO Spending
sections.body:
At the NATO summit, Trump threatened to 'cut off all trade' with Spain over Madrid's refusal to increase defense spending to the alliance's targets. Spain currently spends well below the NATO 2% benchmark, let alone the new 5% by 2035 goal. Trump called Spain a 'wasted cause' and ordered his administration to halt trade, though legal and practical barriers make such a move extremely difficult — trade policy is an exclusive EU competence, and any US action against one member state would trigger a broader EU response.

The threat overshadowed a summit that otherwise celebrated alliance unity and $50 billion in defense deals. European allies pushed back, with multiple leaders stressing the 5% target includes civilian security spending and has a 2035 timeline. The incident highlighted the persistent tension between Trump's transactional approach to burden-sharing and the alliance's consensus-based decision-making.
sections.whyItMatters: Trump's Spain threat is characteristic of his approach to NATO — using commercial leverage to force defense spending compliance — but it also reveals the limits of that approach. The EU's trade competence means the US cannot unilaterally restrict trade with an individual member state without triggering a wider trade conflict. The episode, however, does exert real pressure on low-spending allies and keeps the 5% target in the headlines, which may be the point.

bottomLine: The NATO Ankara summit was the day's defining defense event: over $50 billion in procurement deals, a shift of US weapons production to European soil, a Patriot manufacturing license for Ukraine, and a potential F-35 reversal for Turkey. But the summit was accompanied by developments that illustrate the changing character of military production — Russia's diesel export ban, caused by Ukrainian drone strikes that gutted 25% of its refining capacity, and the Pentagon's solicitation for expendable mass-produced drones to replace $1 billion in lost Reapers. From Australia's new Marine Corps stockpile to India's $119 billion post-Sindoor buying spree, the pattern is the same: the wars in Ukraine and Iran are reshaping not just what countries buy, but where they build it, how fast they need it, and how much they're willing to lose using it.
sources: White House Fact Sheet (July 2026), Reuters, AP News, TASS, Ars Technica, Air Force Times, Air & Space Forces Magazine, Defense Innovation Unit solicitation, Bloomberg, United24 Media, Meduza, AFP, CNBC TV18, The Print, Breaking Defense, USA Today, ABC News, NYT, Lowy Institute, Congressional Research Service
